title: Understand This Puppet-Show Government!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: The Tamil reading copy repairs only source-confirmed or unambiguous OCR spacing and character errors. The canonical Tamil chapter remains unchanged. The extended quotation from The Hindu is preserved in full.

On 21 January 2014, the Tamil Nadu Government issued a "press release" under the heading "Chief Minister Jayalalithaa Orders." In its very first paragraph, it listed two or three measures supposedly being undertaken by the Health Department under this government. But were not all these schemes implemented during the DMK government: the free "108" emergency ambulance service; the insurance scheme for life-saving advanced treatment; the Dr Muthulakshmi Reddy Maternity Financial Assistance Scheme; the Varumun Kappom scheme; the eyesight-restoration scheme; the heart-protection scheme for young children and schoolchildren; the heart-disease prevention scheme; emergency life-saving treatment for newborn babies; and the school health scheme? Did Union Health Minister Ghulam Nabi Azad not openly praise the DMK government, saying, "Tamil Nadu stands as a model for all of India in implementing healthcare programmes"? Can anyone forget that, at a ceremony held in Delhi on 12 April 2010 during the DMK government, the Union Government selected Tamil Nadu as the foremost State for its excellent implementation of healthcare programmes and presented it with an award? Were these not all achievements of the DMK government?

After concealing all this, the government's press release added its crowning claim: that it had decided to open a multi-super-speciality hospital in the finely constructed building at the Omandurar Government Estate, which the DMK government had built for the Secretariat and Legislative Assembly, and that about 27 crore rupees was being spent to convert the building into a hospital.

Neither the Chief Minister nor anyone else speaking for the government explained why a building constructed under the DMK government and used for several months as the Secretariat and Legislative Assembly had to be converted into a hospital. The statement claimed that the building was inaugurated in haste in March 2010 before all construction work had been completed. When large buildings of this kind are inaugurated, it is no defect if a few minor works remain and continue afterwards. From its opening in March 2010 until May 2011, did not the Secretariat and Legislative Assembly function there properly? Was there any delay or problem? Was any work prevented from taking place? Jayalalithaa's stated reason for shifting the Secretariat was that only a few departments were housed in the new complex while buildings for the others were still under construction, and that shifting there would mean transporting files a long distance from the existing Secretariat. But for the past month Chief Minister Jayalalithaa has been governing from Kodanad, six hundred kilometres from Chennai. The Chief Minister herself must explain how it has suddenly become administratively convenient to carry files there and have ministers and officials travel back and forth.

If the Chief Minister wished to establish a multi-super-speciality hospital in Chennai, there were any number of other sites in the city. Had she planned and constructed a building specifically suited to a hospital and opened it there, future Tamil Nadu would remember that this hospital was built and inaugurated while Jayalalithaa was Chief Minister. But what will people say now? They will say that the lady who succeeded Chief Minister Karunanidhi converted into a hospital the Secretariat he had built with immense effort!

The building was constructed under the DMK government for the Secretariat and Legislative Assembly. Solely because Chief Minister Jayalalithaa's animosity led her to convert it into a hospital, the AIADMK government allocated 26 crore and 93 lakh rupees for the conversion, an initial 76 crore and 4 lakh rupees for equipment and furniture, and another 6 crore and 86 lakh rupees to complete the unfinished upper structure and remaining work. It then had an announcement published in every newspaper as an "order of the Chief Minister." How did all this expenditure arise? Instead of spending so much to alter this building, could they not have constructed another building at another location and operated the advanced hospital there? Whose household money is being spent? Can the tax money paid by the people of Tamil Nadu be wasted like this? Who is responsible for all this? Should there not be an inquiry?
Do you know what the English daily The Hindu wrote in an editorial on 16 May 2011, when efforts to abandon this building began within a day or two of the AIADMK taking office?

"During the October Revolution of 1917, some revolutionaries laid siege to the Tsar's palace. The Tsar had stored priceless works of art in that magnificent palace. A connoisseur of art, he had brought together beautiful sculptures, exquisite paintings and ancient monuments from several countries. The revolutionaries who entered, enraged with the Tsar, began smashing those rare artistic treasures. When Comrade Lenin learned of this, he immediately ordered all the revolutionaries to leave the palace. Lenin explained: 'All the artistic treasures in the palace are international monuments of great antiquity, created through human labour. To smash and destroy them would be to destroy with our own hands the symbols of our proud heritage.' The reason for recalling this here is that Chief Minister Jayalalithaa's shifting of the Secretariat and Legislative Assembly from the Omandurar Estate to the old Fort St George brings that incident to mind.

"The new Legislative Assembly building at the Omandurar Estate is said to have been constructed at a cost of about 400 crore rupees. Thousands of workers laboured night and day for many months, shedding their blood and sweat to create it. Many important government departments were moved to the new building. Yet on the third day after the AIADMK won the Assembly election, it decided to move the Secretariat and Legislative Assembly back to the Fort, and reports say that work towards this is now under way. The new Assembly building is not Kalaignar's personal property. It was created with the tax money of the people of Tamil Nadu. It does not seem proper to disregard such a vast building because of personal anger towards Kalaignar. Jayalalithaa has won a resounding victory. The people of Tamil Nadu expect numerous forms of assistance and many programmes from her.

"Displaying personal likes and dislikes in a public matter does not reflect broad-mindedness. It is also said that three or four workers died in accidents before this building was completed. It is distressing to disregard this magnificent building, constructed through the strenuous labour of numerous workers from other States and at the cost of human lives. When a State Chief Minister disregards a building inaugurated by the Prime Minister of the country, it can only be regarded as an insult to the Prime Minister. It is distressing that a Chief Minister should reject, for the sake of one individual, this beautiful building that adds distinction to the city."

That is how The Hindu expressed its anguish in its editorial. Even after the newspaper wrote this, did the Chief Minister correct herself out of respect for such views? How much public money was spent simply because of the obstinate insistence, "I will not go there because the building was constructed by the DMK government"? If Jayalalithaa will not use anything built under a DMK government, she travels every day to the Secretariat by the bridge near the Music Academy, does she not? That bridge too was built during a DMK government. Is it acceptable for her to travel on that alone?

In yesterday's announcement there was yet another declaration: the Chief Minister had apparently sanctioned two crore rupees to appoint field workers and purchase larvicides to eradicate mosquitoes! Two crore rupees to eradicate mosquitoes? If the rubbish lying in Chennai's streets were properly cleared, the mosquitoes would disappear on their own. Can people in Chennai sleep at night? Such is the mosquito menace! The cold season is about to end, and the mosquitoes too will disappear naturally. If the job were to be done properly, it should have been done two months ago; instead, it has been delayed until now. Perhaps the Chief Minister, being away from the capital, does not know of the mosquito menace!

People of Tamil Nadu! Please think! Understand what kind of government you have elected with your votes. The Chief Minister left for Kodanad a month ago! Ask about it, and they say she is governing from there. They say she is returning only today. The Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly is apparently to meet on the 30th! The Governor is apparently going to deliver his address! The Governor's Address is the speech that announces all the government's policies. It is a speech that the Chief Minister must prepare through at least a month of consultation and discussion with the Chief Secretary and the Secretaries of Finance and General Administration. But who is concerned about that speech?

The Chairman of the Minorities Commission has travelled to Udhagamandalam with his family to deliver a wedding invitation. Communist Party of India (Marxist) leaders G. Ramakrishnan, A. Soundararajan and K. Balakrishnan went to Kodanad and met the Chief Minister at the camp office to seek support in the Rajya Sabha election. They have their own concern; what else could they do? The other party, the Communist Party of India, keeps proclaiming every day that it remains in alliance with the AIADMK for the coming parliamentary election. But at her General Council, the Chief Minister thundered that her party would contest all forty seats and then departed. The fishermen's problem has not abated. Even today, twenty-five fishermen have been arrested by the Sri Lankan Navy. The talks scheduled for the 20th have been postponed to the 27th. Ask this government, and it says the talks were postponed at the Union Government's request. Union ministers say that the Tamil Nadu Government caused the delay of a year and a half. Sri Lankan Minister Rajitha Senaratne vows that arrests of Tamil Nadu fishermen will not stop even if the talks take place; arrests continue to this day. The fishermen tried hunger strikes, and now say they will immolate themselves. The threat of a drinking-water shortage looms. A government must attend to all these matters every day and take action. As for law and order, the less said the better. It is openly making itself a laughing stock at every street corner! Is there a day under this government without a murder? Is there a day without a robbery? In the past, chain-snatching incidents occurred here and there. Now newspapers publish round-ups saying, "This many chain-snatching incidents occurred in this locality." This government is run through daily government press releases, video conferences and "remote control." What else is it? Democracy has become a mere object on display. Have you heard of "Tughlaq rule"? If not, look at this puppet-show government and understand!
